schools.summary: Chrysostom: the blessed Paul wishes to show that this covenant is far better than the old, and does so by first laying down remote considerations, for inasmuch as there was nothing bodily or that made a show in the new (no temple, no Holy of Holies, no priest with so great apparel, no legal observances) he establishes the dignity of the priesthood from common principles; Paul defining that every high priest is taken from among men, ordained for men in things pertaining to God, to offer gifts and sacrifices for sins — the general nature of the priestly office as the standard for Christ (Paul, beginning to set forth Christ's priesthood, first laying down what a high priest in general is: he is 'taken from among men,' one of the human race sharing their nature, and 'ordained for men in things pertaining to God,' appointed to act on men's behalf in the things of God's worship and service, the chief part of his work being 'that he may offer both gifts and sacrifices for sins,' to present offerings and sacrifices to God for the sins of the people, so the very nature of the office requires that the priest be a man, acting Godward for men in the matter of sin and sacrifice).

themes: for every high priest taken from among men is ordained for men
in things pertaining to God
that he may offer both gifts and sacrifices for sins
the priestly office as the standard for Christ

schools.summary: Gill: such a high priest can have compassion on those who have committed sins of ignorance and bring their sacrifices for them — he does not insult and upbraid them, nor break out into anger and indignation against them, but pities them and sympathizes with them, having a just measure of compassion suitable to their condition, and bears with their weakness; Paul declaring that the high priest can have compassion on the ignorant and erring, since he himself is compassed with infirmity — the priest's fellow-feeling born of his own weakness (Paul naming a needful quality of the priest: he 'can have compassion on the ignorant, and on them that are out of the way,' deal gently and feel measured pity for those who sin through ignorance or stray, and the reason lying in his own condition, 'for that he himself also is compassed with infirmity,' being himself a weak, sinful man surrounded by the same frailties, the better able to feel for the failings of others, so the earthly priest's very weakness fitted him to sympathize, a thing answered in Christ who shared our infirmity yet without sin).

themes: who can have compassion on the ignorant, and on them that are out of the way
for that he himself also is compassed with infirmity
the priest's fellow-feeling born of his weakness
measured pity for the ignorant and straying

schools.summary: Barnes: because he is a sinner, an imperfect man, as for the people so also for himself, to offer for sins — to make an expiation for sins; he needs the same atonement, and offers the sacrifice for himself which he does for others (Leviticus 9:7); Paul declaring that, by reason of his own infirmity, the priest ought to offer for sins as for the people so also for himself — the earthly priest's own sinfulness requiring sacrifice for himself (Paul drawing out the consequence of the priest's infirmity: 'by reason hereof,' because he too is a sinner compassed with weakness, 'he ought, as for the people, so also for himself, to offer for sins,' the earthly high priest, being himself guilty, having to present a sin-offering for his own sins before he could act for the people, as on the Day of Atonement; this a point of real inferiority in the Aaronic priest, marking the contrast with Christ, who, being sinless, needed no offering for himself but offered only for the people).

debates.question: Why must the earthly priest offer for himself as well as the people?

sides.position: Because he is himself a sinner, compassed with infirmity, he must make expiation for his own sins first — a real inferiority marking the contrast with the sinless Christ, who offered only for the people

themes: and by reason hereof he ought
as for the people, so also for himself, to offer for sins
the earthly priest's own sin requiring sacrifice
a contrast with the sinless Christ

schools.summary: Gill: the office of the high priest was a very honourable one; it was a peculiar honour to Aaron and his sons to be separated unto it, their instalment into it very grand and solemn (anointed with oil and clothed with glorious garments); Paul declaring that no man takes the priestly honour to himself, but he that is called of God, as was Aaron — the priesthood a divine calling, not a self-assumed honour (Paul naming a second requisite of the true priest: the office is 'this honour,' a high and sacred dignity, and 'no man taketh' it 'unto himself,' none may presume to thrust himself into it by his own will, 'but he that is called of God, as was Aaron,' for the priesthood must rest on a divine appointment, even as Aaron was expressly called and consecrated by God, so lawful priesthood depends not on self-assumption but on God's own call, a principle that prepares the way to show that Christ too was divinely called to his office).

themes: and no man taketh this honour unto himself
but he that is called of God, as was Aaron
the priesthood a divine calling
lawful priesthood resting on God's appointment

schools.summary: Gill: it was a glorifying of Christ to make him an high priest, not as God (for as such no addition can be made to his glory, yea it was a condescension in him to become a priest) but as man, it being an honour to the human nature to be united to the Son of God; Paul declaring that Christ glorified not himself to be made high priest, but was appointed by him that said, 'Thou art my Son, today have I begotten thee' — Christ's priesthood received from the Father, not self-assumed (Paul applying the principle to Christ: 'so also Christ glorified not himself to be made an high priest,' he did not of his own will presume to take the priestly office but received it from the Father, even as Aaron was called, and the One who appointed him is 'he that said unto him, Thou art my Son, to day have I begotten thee,' the Father who in the Psalm declared his eternal Sonship, so the same God who owned Christ as his Son also constituted him High Priest, the priesthood resting, as it must, on the Father's call and not on self-assumption).

themes: so also Christ glorified not himself to be made an high priest
he that said unto him, Thou art my Son
to day have I begotten thee
Christ's priesthood received from the Father

schools.summary: Barnes: as he said also in another place (Psalm 110:4), the same God who declared him to be his Son also said 'Thou art a Priest for ever,' the apostle meaning to be understood as saying it is evident here; Paul citing that God said to Christ, 'Thou art a priest for ever after the order of Melchisedec' — the everlasting priesthood of Christ on a higher order than Aaron's (Paul adding a second Scripture to prove Christ's divine call to the priesthood, one that lifts it above the Aaronic: God says to him, 'Thou art a priest for ever after the order of Melchisedec,' not after the order of Aaron, a changing, dying, repeated priesthood, but after the order of Melchizedek, that mysterious king-priest of Genesis; the two great notes here, that Christ's priesthood is 'for ever,' unending and never passing to a successor, and 'after the order of Melchisedec,' of a higher, royal, and abiding kind, a theme Paul will expound at length, marking Christ's priesthood as far surpassing the Levitical).

themes: as he saith also in another place
Thou art a priest for ever
after the order of Melchisedec
an everlasting priesthood of a higher order than Aaron's

schools.summary: Gill: 'the days of his flesh' (or 'of his humanity,' 'when he was clothed with flesh') is the time of his humiliation, when he was attended with the sinless infirmities of the flesh or human nature; it may take in the whole course of his life on earth, especially the time of his sufferings; Paul recalling that Christ, in the days of his flesh, offered prayers and supplications with strong crying and tears to him that was able to save him from death, and was heard in that he feared — the High Priest's agonizing prayers and reverent submission heard by the Father (Paul showing how truly Christ shared our infirmity and learned obedience, pointing to his agony, chiefly in Gethsemane: 'in the days of his flesh,' his earthly suffering life, he 'offered up prayers and supplications with strong crying and tears,' earnest anguished pleadings, 'unto him that was able to save him from death,' to the Father who could deliver him, and 'was heard in that he feared'; this last phrase taken as meaning he was heard because of his reverent, godly fear and submission, the Father hearing and answering him not by sparing him death but by strengthening him through it and raising him from it).

debates.question: How was Christ 'heard in that he feared'?

sides.position: He was heard because of his reverent, godly fear and submission — the Father answered him not by sparing him death but by strengthening him through it and raising him from it

themes: who in the days of his flesh
prayers and supplications with strong crying and tears
unto him that was able to save him from death
heard for his reverent, godly fear

schools.summary: Gill: Christ is 'a Son,' the Son of God (as the Vulgate reads), not by creation, nor by adoption, nor by office, but by nature, being the only begotten of the Father, having the same nature and perfections with him; Paul declaring that, though he were a Son, Christ learned obedience by the things which he suffered — the divine Son experientially learning obedience through suffering (Paul marking the wonder of Christ's humiliation: 'though he were a Son,' the eternal, natural Son of God who might have claimed exemption from suffering, 'yet learned he obedience by the things which he suffered'; this not meaning Christ was ever disobedient or had to be taught to obey, but that he came to know obedience by experience, he who as God commanded now as man practising obedience in the hard school of suffering, learning experimentally what it is to obey God's will through pain and trial, so the Son, in his manhood, was perfected in the actual experience of submissive obedience to the Father).

debates.question: How did the sinless Son 'learn obedience'?

sides.position: Not that he was ever disobedient or needed teaching, but that he came to know obedience by experience — practising in his manhood, through suffering, the submissive obedience he as God commanded

themes: though he were a Son
yet learned he obedience by the things which he suffered
the divine Son learning obedience by experience
obedience practised in the school of suffering

schools.summary: Barnes: 'being made perfect' means being made a complete Saviour, a Saviour fitted in all respects to redeem men, by his sufferings; Gill notes being made perfect in his obedience through sufferings, having completed his obedience, gone through his sufferings, finished his sacrifice, and being perfectly glorified in heaven; Paul declaring that, being made perfect, Christ became the author of eternal salvation to all them that obey him — the perfected Saviour the source of eternal salvation for the obedient (Paul showing the glorious issue of Christ's sufferings and learned obedience: 'being made perfect,' not that he had any imperfection but being completed and consummated as a Saviour, his obedience and sacrifice finished and himself glorified, 'he became the author of eternal salvation,' the cause, source, and procurer of a salvation that is everlasting not temporal like the deliverances of the law, 'unto all them that obey him,' marking the objects of this salvation, those who by faith obey him, the obedience of faith being the mark of those who share the eternal salvation Christ has authored).

themes: and being made perfect
he became the author of eternal salvation
unto all them that obey him
the perfected Saviour the source of everlasting salvation

schools.summary: Gill: there is a resemblance between Melchizedek and Christ, many things said of the one agreeing with the other — there is a likeness in Melchizedek to Christ in his person and what is said of him, that he was without father and mother, without beginning of days or end of life, abiding a priest continually; Paul declaring that Christ was called of God a high priest after the order of Melchisedec — the divine designation of Christ to the Melchizedek priesthood (Paul gathering up the argument of the chapter by repeating Christ's title: he was 'called of God an high priest,' divinely designated to the office as required, 'after the order of Melchisedec,' not after Aaron but after that higher, royal, and abiding order; this verse returning to and sealing the theme of v.6, marking Christ as the God-appointed High Priest of the superior Melchizedek order, in whom the resemblances to that ancient king-priest are fulfilled, a theme Paul is eager to expound but must first rebuke his readers' dullness before he can).

themes: called of God an high priest
after the order of Melchisedec
Christ's divine designation to the Melchizedek priesthood
the God-appointed priest of the higher order

schools.summary: Gill: Paul has many things to say either of Melchizedek, or of Christ, or of his priesthood, or of all of these, since he does largely treat of them in the following chapters (many things of Melchizedek in the seventh chapter, and of Christ and his priesthood in those that follow), but they are 'hard to be uttered,' not in themselves but because of the hearers; Paul saying that he has many things to say, hard to be uttered, because they are dull of hearing — the deep truths hindered not by their difficulty but by the hearers' dullness (Paul, about to launch into the great Melchizedek theme, checking himself to deal with his readers' condition: 'of whom we have many things to say,' much rich teaching about Melchizedek and Christ's priesthood, 'and hard to be uttered,' and naming the true difficulty, not that the truths are too obscure in themselves but 'seeing ye are dull of hearing,' the hearers having grown sluggish and slow in spiritual understanding so that the deep things could not easily be made plain to them, so the fault lay not in the doctrine but in their dull, untaught state).

themes: of whom we have many things to say, and hard to be uttered
seeing ye are dull of hearing
the deep truths hindered by the hearers' dullness
the fault in their slow state, not the doctrine

schools.summary: Gill: these Hebrews had had great advantages — they were not only descended from Abraham and had the law of Moses and the writings of the Old Testament, but some of them had enjoyed the ministry of Christ and of his apostles, and it was now about thirty years from the day of Pentecost; Paul rebuking that, when they ought to be teachers, they need to be taught again the first principles, having need of milk not strong meat — the reproof of arrested spiritual growth (Paul charging them with a shameful backwardness: 'when for the time ye ought to be teachers,' after so long in the faith they should have grown able to instruct others, 'ye have need that one teach you again which be the first principles of the oracles of God,' having to be re-taught the very ABC of the faith, 'and are become such as have need of milk, and not of strong meat'; the figure, milk being the elementary easy doctrines fit for babes and 'strong meat' the deeper truths fit for the mature, so that, instead of advancing, they had slipped back to spiritual infancy, needing milk when they should have been feeding on solid food).

themes: for when for the time ye ought to be teachers
ye have need that one teach you again which be the first principles
are become such as have need of milk, and not of strong meat
the reproof of arrested spiritual growth

schools.summary: Gill: every one that useth milk — and sits down contented with the first principles of the gospel, such as are easily taken in and digested, or makes use of the ceremonial law as a schoolmaster to teach him the gospel — is unskilful in the word of righteousness; Barnes refers it to the food of children; Paul declaring that everyone who uses milk is unskilful in the word of righteousness, for he is a babe — the spiritual infancy of those who feed only on first principles (Paul explaining the figure: 'every one that useth milk,' whoever can take in only the simplest, most elementary teaching, 'is unskilful in the word of righteousness,' unpractised, inexpert, lacking discernment in the deeper doctrine of the gospel, the word that reveals the righteousness of God in Christ, and giving the reason, 'for he is a babe,' a spiritual infant undeveloped and untaught, so to remain content with milk is to remain a babe, unfit for and unskilled in the weightier truths of the faith).

themes: for every one that useth milk is unskilful in the word of righteousness
for he is a babe
the spiritual infancy of the milk-fed
unfit for the deeper word of righteousness

schools.summary: Gill: 'strong meat' belongs to them that are of full age, or perfect — this intends not a perfection of justification (for though some have a greater degree of faith than others and a clearer discovery of their justification, babes in Christ are as perfectly justified as more grown and experienced believers), but a maturity of spiritual growth and understanding; Paul declaring that strong meat belongs to the mature, who by use have their senses exercised to discern good and evil — the deeper truths fit for the spiritually mature and discerning (Paul completing the figure with its bright side: 'strong meat belongeth to them that are of full age,' the deeper, weightier doctrines of the gospel being the proper food of the spiritually mature, the grown men in Christ, 'even those who by reason of use have their senses exercised to discern both good and evil'; the mark of maturity here not mere age but a trained spiritual discernment, gained 'by reason of use,' by habitual practice and exercise of the faculties of the soul, so the mature can tell sound from unsound, truth from error, good from evil, so growth in grace is shown by an exercised, discerning judgment, fit to feed on the solid food of the deeper truths).

themes: but strong meat belongeth to them that are of full age
who by reason of use have their senses exercised
to discern both good and evil
growth shown by a trained, discerning judgment
